summary: Soviet composer (1934–1998) of Jewish Volga German ancestry, considered the foremost Soviet composer after Shostakovich. Known for 'polystylism' — the mixing of contrasting musical idioms, from tango to pastiche — across 8 symphonies, 6 concerti grossi, and many other works. His music was suppressed by the Soviet government. Champion violinist Gidon Kremer was a major advocate.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Soviet composer of Jewish Volga German ancestry, considered the foremost Soviet composer after Shostakovich. His music was suppressed by the Soviet regime. Known for 'polystylism,' which he theorized in a 1971 lecture in Moscow. Suffered a severe stroke in 1985 (and more thereafter); his gravestone has a fermata over a rest marked fff. Violinist Gidon Kremer was his major champion. Wrote 8 symphonies, 6 concerti grossi, and composed music for animated films. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Foremost Soviet composer after Shostakovich; his music was suppressed by the Soviet government
examples: This man of Jewish Volga German ancestry with 'dissident-length' hair mixed pastiche with various styles in his first of 8 symphonies, calling it 'polystylism.'
For 10 points, Alfred Schnittke's music was suppressed by the government of what country that aggressively promoted Prokofiev and Shostakovich?

clues.clue: Known for 'polystylism' — mixing contrasting musical idioms (U and E, 'entertaining' and 'serious' music); theorized in a 1971 Moscow lecture
examples: This polystylist composer of six concerti grossi also wrote three cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto.
In a lecture given in Moscow, Alfred Schnittke theorized that 20th century composers ranging from Webern to Part tended towards this aesthetic, which is similar to eclecticism.

clues.clue: Gidon Kremer was a major advocate and commissioner of his works
examples: Gidon Kremer commissioned his 1977 Concerto Grosso No. 1 and championed his 2 postmodern cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto.
Name this composer whose advocate Gidon Kremer recorded his controversial modernist cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto.

clues.clue: Suffered a severe stroke in 1985; his gravestone has a fermata over a rest marked fff
examples: His gravestone has a fermata over a rest marked fff.
Complications from that composer's 1985 stroke inspired him to use an even more jarring and fragmented version of his typical 'polystylism.'

Known for such 'polystylistic' works as his ballet Peer Gynt and his Faust Cantata.

examples: Alfred Schnittke scored a surreal animated film titled for this instrument that was banned in the USSR.

examples: A work for two violins and orchestra by Schnittke puns on the name of these two composers, the most prominent of the Classical era.

works.name: Concerto Grosso No. 1
works.description: Commissioned by and dedicated to Gidon Kremer (1977). A polystylist double violin concerto / concerto grosso calling for two solo violins, a prepared piano (opened with coins/kopeks in the Preludio), and a harpsichord. The fifth movement (Rondo) features a 'banal' E-flat minor tango on harpsichord. Mixes film music parody and Tchaikovsky Violin Concerto pastiche. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The Preludio first movement opens with a prepared piano passage (piano prepared with kopeks/coins) in thirds
examples: An amplified piano prepared with coins to sound like bells opens that piece by this composer in thirds.

A prepared piano solo begins the first of six compositions of this type by Alfred Schnittke.

works.name: Cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto
works.description: Schnittke wrote controversial polystylist cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto, championed by Gidon Kremer. The first cadenza quotes from Bartók's two violin concerti and Shostakovich's first violin concerto; another quotes from Alban Berg. Most soloists prefer Fritz Kreisler's cadenzas instead. Wikipedia

Schnittke's first cadenza to the Beethoven Concerto includes quotations from this man's two violin concerti.

works.name: Cello Concerto No. 1
works.description: Schnittke finished his Cello Concerto No. 1 after his first severe stroke. The largely stratospheric and fortissimo coda requires the soloist to be amplified. A more jarring 'polystylist' style resulted from his stroke. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Finished after his first severe stroke; the coda is largely stratospheric and fortissimo, requiring the soloist to be amplified
examples: He finished his grim Cello Concerto No. 1 after his first severe stroke.

In another cello concerto from this country, a largely stratospheric and fortissimo coda requires the soloist to be amplified; complications from that composer's 1985 stroke inspired him to use an even more jarring and fragmented version of his typical 'polystylism.'

comprehensive_summary:
Alfred Schnittke (1934–1998) was a Soviet composer of Jewish Volga German ancestry, widely considered the foremost Soviet composer after Shostakovich. Known for his long 'dissident-length' hair and his provocative mixing of musical styles, his music was suppressed by the Soviet regime. He formed part of the 'Big Troika' of modernist Soviet composers alongside Sofia Gubaidulina and Edison Denisov.

Schnittke's most famous theoretical contribution is 'polystylism' — the juxtaposing and intersecting of contrasting musical idioms within a single work, ranging from Baroque pastiche to tango to film music. He theorized this approach in a 1971 lecture presented in Moscow. His gravestone famously features a fermata over a rest marked fff. He suffered a severe stroke in 1985 and composed the Cello Concerto No. 1 after this, using an even more jarring and fragmented polystylist style. He wrote 8 symphonies, 6 concerti grossi, and film music including a score for a Soviet animated film about a glass harmonica that was banned by authorities.

His Concerto Grosso No. 1 (1977), commissioned by violinist Gidon Kremer, is his most frequently clued work. It calls for two solo violins, a harpsichord, and a piano prepared with kopeks (coins) in the opening Preludio movement. The Toccata features two violins in a frantic cascading stretto canon. The fifth movement Rondo contains a 'banal' E-flat minor tango on harpsichord, offering brief respite from the cacophonous parody of Tchaikovsky's Violin Concerto and Schnittke's own film music. The composer described it as mixing 'U' and 'E' music — entertaining and serious.

Schnittke also wrote controversial polystylist cadenzas for Beethoven's Violin Concerto, championed and recorded by Gidon Kremer. The first cadenza quotes from Bartók's two violin concerti and Shostakovich; another quotes from Alban Berg. Most soloists prefer Fritz Kreisler's cadenzas. His Third String Quartet opens with three explicitly labeled quotations of Orlando di Lasso, Beethoven, and the DSCH motif. His Quasi una sonata (Violin Sonata No. 2) uses the BACH motif in a passacaglia movement and quotes 'La Cucaracha' in the finale. His Symphony No. 3 uses an eight-tone row spelling out 'das Böse' ('the evil'). His polystylist choral Faust Cantata ends with a wild tango movement 'Es geschah' accompanying Faust's death. He also composed the ballet Peer Gynt and the work Moz-Art à la Haydn for two violins and orchestra.

cards.front: Composer: foremost Soviet composer after Shostakovich; Jewish Volga German ancestry; his music was suppressed by the Soviet government; gravestone has a fermata over a rest marked fff
Composer: coined 'polystylism' — mixing contrasting musical idioms from Baroque pastiche to tango; theorized in a 1971 Moscow lecture
Composer: wrote a Faust Cantata ending with a wild tango 'Es geschah' accompanying Faust's death; also wrote a ballet Peer Gynt
Composer: Symphony No. 3 uses an eight-tone row spelling out 'das Böse' ('the evil')
Composer: Third String Quartet opens with three explicitly-labeled quotations of Orlando di Lasso, Beethoven, and the DSCH motif
Composer: Quasi una sonata (Violin Sonata No. 2) — BACH motif in the passacaglia third movement; finale main theme based on 'La Cucaracha'
Concerto Grosso: Preludio first movement opens with an amplified piano prepared with kopeks (coins); the Rondo features a 'banal' E-flat minor tango on harpsichord
Concerto Grosso: Toccata movement features two solo violins in a frantic cascading stretto canon; commissioned by Gidon Kremer (1977)

Concerto: controversial polystylist cadenzas for this Beethoven work championed by Gidon Kremer; the first cadenza quotes Bartók and Shostakovich; another quotes Alban Berg; most soloists use Fritz Kreisler's cadenzas instead
Concerto: finished after the composer's first severe stroke; the coda is largely stratospheric and fortissimo, requiring the soloist to be amplified
